sentences.text: Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Under the Russians, we manage.
We manage.
We got-- I got land, and the land was putting in potatoes, my own potatoes.
I got mine own rye, and we survive.
We put in, always, when we come in before the winter, we put in bowls with pickles and sauerkraut.
What do you call the-- cabbage.
And when it come winter, we-- we survive.
When-- and later, they give me a job, even, the Russian.
I was buying like-- I was buying cattle-- not buying-- collecting cattle for them.
In the meantime, when I was collecting cattle, then I-- then I have a chance to sell a cattle for mine life, for-- for surviving too, or care for something.
And it was-- we survive.
We don't get rich, but we survive.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Itzchak-- Itzchak was still in Kolki.
He's still in Kolki.
And they have a next-- three or four blocks away-- with a-- with a family.
He got a big family.
He got three, four kids.
And-- and he have grocery too.
And you know, grocery was surviving, too.
Before the Russian.
He even--
No, I'm talking after the Russians came.
After Russian, he kept it, but later he closed.
They giving so much problem that he have to close.
After that-- after that, when the-- when the-- when the German come in--
No, not-- not the German.
I don't want to get there, yet.
By the Russian.
Yeah, we were in Matejko.
Matejko.
And-- and--
Right.
In Kolki.
And Mayer, the younger brother, was with me.
Yeah, Matejko.
I was growing potatoes, and I helping out for the other-- for the other son, for the other brother.
I give them potatoes and cabbage.
And we help each other.
It's like one family.
No.
If-- he using for himself.
That's for using.
Yeah, yes, yes.
And I get a check from them and that, and I can--
--survive too.
And there was-- they have-- they have stores, like everybody stay in line and buy something.
Then you have to have the rubles.
And I have some rubles-- make some rubles.
And we stay in line.
Sometime we wake up in the morning and middle of the night to get first.
Because you come late, you couldn't get it.
And we survive.
You don't have other choice.
You got to survive.
Oh, yeah.
We was doing.
Yeah.
Well, it's in the house.
Nobody was after us-- nobody.
We got the-- we got the old Bibles, and the Chumash, and everything.
We do it in the house.
There was no synagogue in Matejko.
There was no synagogue anyway.
Well, they change when-- when the-- when the German come in-- when the Germans.
1941, they come in, and they start, right away.
They start, right away, killing-- in Kolki.
They start right away, killing in Kolki.
We been in Matejko.
We been in Matejko, but we hear what, every day they come in.
They brought it to us, and we hear what happened.
And they start killing.
And they killing, and-- and they taken for work and for-- something to work, and this, and that-- and the killing.
Every day they're killing.
There they-- they lying.
They was taking like 20-- 20, 30 of the best men from town, to work.
And they take them there, and they kill them.
And the women still was thinking they're alive.
They was thinking they're alive.
And later, they put them all together in a ghetto-- all together, everybody in a ghetto.
Is mine older brother and the little boy run away from the ghetto, to-- to us-- to come to us-- to Matejko.
Yeah, and-- and Duvid, the-- the brother-in-law, and mine-- and my sister, Leake-- they run away, too.
They come to us.
To Matejko.
Then we been Matejko-- what he lost-- his wife and the kids.
They take them-- everybody-- to killed.
And Itzchak-- Itzchak-- yeah-- the whole-- the kids
His wife and the kids, they took.
Yeah, they took and they killed them-- all of them.
They been shot, sure.
They take them in the-- they take them in the woods-- they call [NON-ENGLISH], the wood.
And they were-- they-- they dig, himself the-- the-- the holes, and they kill them They were saying later-- I hear they're saying even a lot of was not even killed.
It was the-- the ground was-- was awaken-- shaking-- the ground.
They put in just like that.
And that's was the end.
After that, they come to us in Matejko.
In Matejko, we was-- we had plenty of food.
I have a-- I have a farm.
I have everything.
And then they come in.
They tell us to go.
Not the Germans, the-- the-- the-- from that same town.
Yeah, they're--
Oh, people-- people from Kolki.
--crying-- they're crying.
And he was the head of the-- of the town.
He tell us, Louis, you got to go.
The Ukrainian, yeah.
We have a message to go.
Then we went to Osowa-- not too far, Osowa.
Osowa.
There was a Jewish-- a Jewish-- a Jewish pharmacy-- Jewish people.
Yeah, right.
Osowa, they went to Osowa.
We went to Osowa.
We have, there-- we have there Duvid's, my brother's-in-law father there, and the mother, and everything.
We went to their house.
Everybody-- Itzchak and every-- and Itzchak's little boy, and-- and me and my wife.
I was married, too.
I don't even tell you.
I was a-- later, I was married.
I was married-- it's a funny thing,
I was married one week before the German.
1941, then my wife was with me, too, in Matejko.
Anna-- Anna.
And-- and then we went, everybody, to that place.
And finally, they-- we was saying they wouldn't kill farmers, because they killed [NON-ENGLISH], where they do business, or they don't work out.
But this was all farmers-- farms.
Everything is farms, farmers.
Maybe the whole town must have one grocery or two.
Then we-- they were saying the German wouldn't kill that.
They kill just other-- other Jewish people, not Jewish people what they farmers.
Because they show in the end, they're working.
Everybody's working.
People--
The-- the people wouldn't believe that.
I don't believe they believed that.
You have people that say there was not a Holocaust, even-- some people saying.
They wouldn't believe that.
Then we come there, and we live there.
We survive there-- survive, survive, survive.
Soon-- at a time when the German is here-- when German is here, you run.
You run from the house.
Osowa, they-- they always was there, [NON-ENGLISH], like police or crime police.
And the-- and the German come to the crime police and ask.
And they want money.
They want gold.
They want this.
They want that.
OK.
And they went away.
Oh, but in the meantime, when they come in, you dead.
You're-- you're not [NON-ENGLISH].
You don't know where to go.
You don't-- you know there was farm there.
There was a lot of-- all kind of trees, there.
Then you hide there, and you see the other.
They went, and you go back to the house.
In the night, you couldn't get out.
The crime police wouldn't let you out.
You have to stay in the house, in the night.
In the daytime, they let you go.
You can go in the daytime.
Is-- when they-- then we staying.
And we was thinking, we gonna be survive there, right?
We gonna be survive.
All of a sudden, they come in one time.
They Germans, or--
Yeah, in the meantime--
No, in the meantime--
--the four other guards-- we was there, like in a ghetto.
Also it was a ghetto-- everybody in one package.
And they take us to Kostopol lager to work.
I Forgot to tell you the main thing.
Kostopol lager-- work.
Work was all kind of work to-- it was-- we-- we was digging turf.
We was putting the big trees on the-- on the trains.
No, no.
They come in with the trains.
And they-- like the telephone poles.
We have to put them in.
And he was saying, always, with that Ukraine-- it was not the Germans.
It was Ukrainians.
Ukrainians ran-- they ran the camp.
And always, even you do good, everything is still hit you.
With a whip-- with a-- with a big whip-- still hit you.
And they go all over, throw you even in the face-- sometime turn it over.
Oh!
If I-- if I get a day not whipped, I was lucky.
There not such a day.
You get whipped.
I don't know why he was doing.
We was trying-- when we come back, they keep us four weeks.
We couldn't come back.
We was dead.
They give us-- no, no.
They give us, in the ghetto, a place to stay.
And every morning, we have to go to work.
Well, everything, walk, walk.
Oh, no, no driving-- none-- walking.
And walking, we was walking on-- not on-- not on the sidewalk-- where the horses, where the-- where the wagons going.
And then everybody is looking at us.
Oh, where-- we went-- from Osowa to Kostopol it was, let's see-- like two hours ride with horses-- two hours ride-- no, two hour-- one hour with a car.
Oh, no.
We walk one time to stay there.
And they keep us four weeks there.
And after that, we walk back home, yeah.
Back-- back to Osowa.
And then, we always thinking they wouldn't bother us-- work, not work, we cutting in the wood, the-- the trees.
Everything we give it in along we can be survive-- be alive.
Over there, then they come in.
Some day they come in, and they took us all together.
And then--
The German with Ukraines-- it was a lot.
You know, everybody have to get out from their houses.
And they-- and they keep us walking to Kostopol, to that-- like in that-- in that way.
Like we gone.
Then we-- we-- we lucky.
We-- we-- we run away, like, we was-- our house was the last one in that Osowa-- the last house.
And right away, was the woods-- but ours.
And when we hear that, we don't do nothing.
We went out, and we ran away.
And we lucky.
We-- we lucky we ran out, that time.
And the rest, everybody take them away.
A lot run away, and a lot they killed.
And so-- so it's finish.
It was finished.
It was over.
We was not with them.
Well, they killed.
You didn't have to see.
Anybody was killed.
There was coming in, maybe, maybe run away, another 7, 8, 10 people-- there, from them.
Well, it was my brother, the older brother.
It was the younger brother, my wife, and there was another girl, another three boys-- friend too with us.
We ran away, like, all together, like 12 people.
The parents was passing away in that Osowa-- passing away.
And we brought them to the right place.
Natural, natural die in that Osowa.
Right at the court-- buried in the--
--in the cemetery, right.
I remember the mother, me and mine two brothers, and my brother-in-law.
We carried the mother.
It was very religious.
And the father was pass away in four days-- in four weeks--
--after that.
And we-- we put them away, even one next to the other one-- on the cemetery in Osowa.
This-- this I remember.
They're lucky.
They lucky, what they pass away.
They lucky.
Then we went to the woods.
When we--
After we ran away.
We went to the woods.
We see in the woods.
You have to eat.
Then we went in that-- Matejko.
Matejko got a lot of good-- good people, too-- not all of them, but I got good people there-- where I can trust him.
Ukraine-- Ukraine.
I can trust them, because I was-- I have the grocery there.
And I help him a lot.
And we was friend-- like friend-- everything friend.
And I left him a lot of things there.
I left them there, say, maybe sometime I come.
He help me out.
And some places, I couldn't go, because they be-- kill me.
Because like now, one place I have, I know I'm sure there they wouldn't kill me.
They don't go for the thing.
And I was getting there, getting some in change for bread.
Change for bread-- I got some clothes.
I give away shoes.
I got other things give away.
And I get, always, bread from the people.
And I get flour from the people-- I always, to-- to bake bread.
I got a good-- no, I got a woman, too.
She bake-- she bake me the bread.
She bake me-- even put in potatoes.
There was more bread with potatoes.
And it was taste good.
Later, she don't want to do it.
She was afraid.
She said, Louis, I can't do it.
I'm afraid they're-- they're gonna kill me if they find out.
Then-- then we don't-- we don't bake bread, because we don't have where to bake.
That was very hard.
And then, we see if he stay-- if he stay by a good friend, a Ukrainitz, are there, right?
OK, he help us.
Then he say, Louis, we can't.
The police goes right there.
He's was in-- on the road, and the police was right away driving.
I mean it, he came in to kill him, and he kill us, you know?
That's what they're saying.
Then we say, we don't have other choice, what to do.
Then we find out we gonna do two bunkers in a wood-- in the woods.
This was between Matejko and Kolki.
We find out a very, very tight woods with a lot of woods.
And we went there.
And we make two bunkers.
Six-- six people was in one bunker, and six people was in the other bunker.
Underground, yeah.
Just, we dig it up, and cover up.
And we were staying there.
And-- and me and mine brother, we was-- the smaller brother was like the strongest.
We always have empty bottles with-- with us for water, sometime milk, we get in.
We get it.
And was the runners and the bringers.
We was the-- everything for survive for the other-- for mine other brother, the older one, and my wife, and-- and-- us-- in-- and the brother-in-law.
They was like more-- more religious, and more-- not active, like we was active.
And we was active.
We was active.
We were surviving and helping.
No, no.
We don't get no kosher.
Everything was kosher.
You don't get nothing not kosher.
They gonna give you-- they give you-- what they gonna give you a pig or something?
Nobody will give you nothing.
You're lucky you get a-- sometime a bottle of milk or a piece of bread.
Into the woods, because we see--
Yeah, you have to go only to good people where you know them.
Not-- you can't go to everybody.
Because if they go to everybody, they say right away, I saw Louis.
I saw him with-- I see with Mayer, you know.
Then they start looking for you.
This way, you try to hide.
And we was going only in the days when the moon was not the moon.
Once a month, when-- when everything is dark.
When it's light, I never go with Mayer.
Always we go when it's dark.
And then nobody see you.
And you-- you can get through.
That's when we was going.
And we survive.
And we don't-- when we get up-- pulled up-- I get like 60 pounds.
They call them a pit in Europe-- a flour-- not flour-- it's a wheat-- wheat-- wheat where you can cook them and eat them.
And I don't know.
We were strong.
We still were strong from the air or something-- from the cold weather.
That's what we eating.
When we have our-- our 60 pounds, that wheat, then we surviving.
We have something to eat.
Like, like, let's say China eat rice, and we eat the-- and we eat that wheat.
And it was good.
They don't ever to hide.
We were staying on-- on the roof-- mostly on the roof.
We can't get in.
We stayed there on the roof.
And mostly, they have some shacks in the back, we were staying.
They're afraid to keep us in the house.
That-- that was not keep on in the house.
I heard some people, sometimes they hid in the barns with animals.
That was good.
That's where we went after that.
After the woods.
After the woods, we went there.
In the woods, we was a long time in the woods.
We was a long time.
I think we were holding up a good couple months.
After that-- after that we hear shooting-- shooting.
We shoot.
And this is winter-- winter, and it was so strong, the snow, and the-- maybe below-- 10 below degree.
I don't know.
It was so cold.
No, I used to have-- I always wear-- I wear a shoe-- Ukrainian shoe-- not-- not regular shoe, because regular shoes, they-- they find out you walking.
Then the Jew-- then a Jew was walking.
They-- they go right to you.
And I have-- and I have a cover, a-- a Ukrainian cover.
And on the bottom I have like from sheep, that skin.
And that was warm for me.
It was warm, and my wife, the same thing.
And everybody, we survive.
Oh, but this not-- when the point is, we hear shooting.
And we was 12 people there-- 6 and 6.
And one shooter is right near a horse.
And we were still sitting.
At that time they-- they were never finding when we-- when we have two boys, where they jump out from the-- from the bunker.
And they jump out, and they come.
And we shooting from this side, and he jump out, they went there.
Then we start, everybody go out from that bunker-- to go out.
And I steady-- I-- I never forget that day of my life.
That was seven days before Hanukkah-- seven days before Hanukkah was that.
And the murderers find the place.
They find our place, the murderers, what they were looking for us-- the murderers.
Murder.
Right.
The German with Ukraine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went-- I went-- let's see.
Shooting from this-- and I went this way.
I went this way.
Mine brother, the younger brother, why he went that way?
Everybody went that way-- close.
And they got him.
How they get the other bunker?
The other bunker was far away, and they got the other bunker.
And I-- in that time-- and I went this way.
I went this way.
I know the-- I know the neighbor over there.
I know him, because I lived there.
I know the neighbor.
I was driving to Kolki, Matejko-- I always know it.
And I went this way, and there was very, very strong snow, like, like crystal-- the snow and the-- and the everything, I'm telling you.
And I was walking-- and keep my bottle here, the empty bottle.
And that's it.
And I figure, maybe that's a wood.
I think, should we run away?
I was thinking.
In the meantime, I see two men there cutting-- cutting trees.
And I see they going after me.
They going.
Then I start talking to them in Ukrainish.
I say, do you need me?
Where you coming?
Do you need me?
And I took like, to the bottle.
They thinking maybe I have something.
And finally, they listen to me.
They went back to cut-- to cut the trees.
And I went straight.
When I went straight, they always looking for you, where you walking.
And I have these shoes, the-- the Ukraine shoes.
They can-- then I went on the road.
And with the road, I went with the shoe.
And then I went on the left side, and I came in the wood.
And there I stop in the wood.
The wood was not, no-- no leaves.
It was still wood.
And I was staying there.
And I hear shooting, shooting.
By yourself.
I'm only one there.
And they shooting.
And they shooting.
And I hear a shoot right near me.
I don't see nobody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I was saying to God that I am actually lived so-- two times in my life I talk to God.
And God help me.
I know that.
There was real talking to God, that time.
And he helped me.
And-- and I say to God, just like this-- I say-- I say, God, you help me.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- I want nothing.
I want just one suit for holiday and one suit for every day.
And I don't care what-- what to eat, just if you let me live.
And I say to God, please make dark.
It was very light.
I couldn't go in the night to see what going on.
It was very light.
I say, make [NON-ENGLISH]-- make-- make dark.
And finally, I was waiting into the night.
I was waiting to late in the night.
And we have-- we have a signal.
If somebody get lost or something, and-- and look-- to come in one place to meet each other.
Then I-- I went to the place to meet you all-- who is-- maybe somebody survive-- come to the place.
When I come to the place, I couldn't get in, in that place.
I say, something wrong.
In that place was a man live with a Jewish girl, in Osowa.
And-- and he promise-- he promise.
So he-- she gonna survive.
He gonna-- and she's-- I was coming there.
And she says to me, Louis, I have to die.
I say-- I say nothing.
This is life.
You've got to survive.
She was leave with him in the night.
And she even get pregnant from him.
And he was keep on the day there, in the back, in a room.
Ukraine and Jewish girl-- Ukraine.
Then, well, he was good to me.
And he was-- we can trust him, because if you figure.
Then I come to him-- and I couldn't-- I'm afraid to get in.
I know what I'm-- and then I get in.
I get him.
And he says, that-- there was your-- the-- the police.
And they kill that girl, and they want to kill him, too.
And I say, I'm-- I'm in bad shape.
And I run away.
And I run away, and I see nobody is there-- nobody there.
When I come tomorrow, I went to another friend, to a-- to Ukrainians, and find out what going on.
He wouldn't kill me, never.
He's very good-- Maxime.
That was a good friend for all the time there, when I been a mate.
And he tell me, they kill all of them.
How come, Louis-- Louis, you live?
Louis you live.
They killed everybody that was in your group?
They think they kill me, too.
They killed everybody.
They tie them up.
And my sister was asking them to take her to the cemetery, to Kolk.
And then she always bring-- ask him to the cemetery, to the Kolk-- Kolki, you know, but they don't listen.
And everybody killed.
And they killed me, too.
They think they killed me, too.
And all of a sudden, I'm alive.
Then he says, that means something.
Then he ask me, where is Mayer?
Because he always know I go with Mayer together.
I say, Mayer, there is little-- little, but he's all right.
He's all right.
I don't say Mayer-- I don't say Mayer is killed.
Because Mayer--
Yeah, he was shot.
But to me, he was my protection.
When I get in to Ukrainians to get bread, or something, even I trust him, he have to know Mayer is alive, outside.
Because the other way, he can kill me, too, you know.
You can't trust nobody.
You don't know it.
And I always go with, him.
Mayer is alive.
And they believe me, because they say they kill me, and I'm alive.
Is Mayer alive too?
Then-- then after that, I see everything is done.
And I went-- and my wife that time, the first wife, Anna, was by a girlfriend, a Polish girlfriend.
She was hiding there by--
She was not with me, because she was very weak.
And sometime, the girlfriend take her, and she washed there.
And she was there with her.
Then I went back to her, to my wife-- first wife.
She was-- first she was hiding on the roof, there, with a lot of hay.
That was warm there.
And they was taking good care of-- a Polish girl-- girlfriend was taking--
This was in Krasnowola-- Krasnowola.
It's not too far from Matejko.
And we have-- and she have friend there, like Polish-- Polish-- Polish girl.
And we were staying there.
And we were staying there.
And then we see on the roof, is no place to-- there was another Polish man, with another girl.
And he make us-- he make us a room under the sheep.
He have a-- he have a-- like a-- for the cows-- a place for the cows-- four apartments.
I call it four apartments, four-- four--
Stalls-- a cow here, and a cow here, and a cow there, and the fourth one is the sheeps.
And he make us a place hidden league under the sheeps.
And we could just sit in and-- and lay in.
And I was bringing-- I was always bringing bread, once a month, bringing him.
And he was feeding us.
And it was very good.
Then we were with them-- we was there a long time too-- a good-- good months there, we laying like that.
And I always go when the moon is dark, I go to Matejko, and get always, to bring up, 60 pounds that flour.
And he was baking, and he bring us.
And at one time, I say to my wife, take a look.
The sheeps get food.
We don't.
The sheeps was feeding in the morning-- oh, but not us.
And all of a sudden, in the meantime, they get-- the Polish people and the Ukrainian, they start fighting, between them.
Then the Polish people start fighting with the Ukrainian.
And they-- and they was Polish people-- the place where I was.
And they come after them.
And one time, her daughter come to our bunker-- hide with us together.
It was so bad, already.
And we see that's another place.

And the-- and the-- and the Ukrainian was go-- coming in the-- come in there, in the-- in the bars-- not in the barn there, in bars-- in the bar-- in the stalls with the cattle, and with the sticks, looking for something on the floor.
Looking-- they know that I-- that I sometime-- maybe clothes or something.
I know what they want.
And they come in-- there come in a guy with a stick.
And he says, [NON-ENGLISH]-- mean cow, cow.
And he sticks.
Then he come in.
Then he come in the other room.
